Recall that we've said that the important amplifier of authority perceived credibility has two components, expertise, that is, knowledge on the topic, and trustworthiness, that is honesty. As we've seen, expertise is a powerful, even necessary feature of credibility. What studies have demonstrated that it's not enough to establish you as an entirely credible communicator. Even experts won't be fully credible and thus maximally persuasive unless they're also viewed as offering their expert information in an honest, unbiased fashion. Let's take an illustration from the world of financial investments. Sam Bankman Freed, a renowned expert in the trading of cryptocurrency, is often called the modern-day version of Bernie Madoff, the well-known financial derivatives expert who swindled his investors out of billions of dollars with such a Madoff-like reputation. Would you trust Mr. Bankman Freed's recommendation to invest your retirement portfolio in his cryptocurrency companies? Despite the fact that he's an acknowledged expert on the topic, I guess not.
